title: Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List

statement.markdown: Given a doubly linked list, implement a function mergeSort that merges two sorted doubly linked lists into a single sorted list. The function should work by recursively splitting the list into two halves until each sublist only contains one node, then reversing the sublists to form the final sorted doubly linked list.
statement.inputFormat: Head of doubly linked list in form of a ListNode object: {val: x, prev: null, next: null}
statement.outputFormat: Head of merged doubly linked list in form of a ListNode object: {val: x, prev: null, next: null}
statement.notes: Merge two sorted doubly linked lists in reverse order (latest first).

examples.input: {val: 2, next: {val: 4, prev: null, next: null}, prev: null}
examples.explanation: Input doubly linked list is 2 -> 4, mergeSort returns 4 -> 2.

hints.text: Identify the problem with the buggy implementation.
Consider how you can merge two sorted lists in reverse order.

editorial.title: Understanding merge sort on doubly linked list
editorial.intuition: To merge two sorted doubly linked lists, you must recursively split the list into two halves until each sublist only contains one node, then reverse the sublists to form the final sorted doubly linked list.
editorial.bruteForce: Merge two sorted doubly linked lists using a two-pointer approach and reverse the lists afterwards.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a recursive approach to split the doubly linked lists into two halves, then merge the sublists in reverse order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved using mathematical induction on the recursion depth.
editorial.dryRun: Dry run the solution with a small input doubly linked list to verify its correctness.
